docs.title: About
docs.text:
Personal site

I am Sameer Sharma.

This site is where I publish essays, technical notes, and longer-form writing that I want to keep in one place.

docs.title: Posts
docs.text:
Research, engineering, and fiction

This is the index for everything I publish here. The site is organized into collections so readers can follow a long-running research project, browse standalone technical notes, or read creative work without those modes collapsing into each other.

Research implementation 100 Days of Research One paper at a time: implementation, reproduction, results, and takeaways. Engineering notes Technical Notes Practical notes across machine learning, software, systems, and tools. Creative diary Short Stories Fiction and reflective fragments kept close to the technical notebook.

docs.title: Current Highlights
docs.text: Research · Reproducibility Day 001: Paper Implementation Template The repeatable format I use to turn a paper into an implementation and public write-up. Computer Vision · Geometry Monocular depth for real-world measurement Turning a single image into approximate metric measurements with calibration, geometry, and depth estimation. Python · Packaging Are you __init__ or not? A short field guide to package layout choices in Python projects.

docs.title: Publishing Guide
docs.text:
How I keep this site organized

This page is the operating manual for the blog. It keeps collections, tags, and post formats consistent as the site grows.

docs.title: Collections
docs.text:
Use one of these folders for every new post:

  • docs/posts/research-100/ for paper implementations and reproduction logs
  • docs/posts/technical-notes/ for engineering notes, tutorials, debugging notes, and opinions
  • docs/posts/short-stories/ for fiction and reflective writing

docs.title: Tag Vocabulary
docs.text:
Tags are shown inside each post with a tag-row. Keep them short and reusable.

Recommended technical tags:

  • Machine Learning
  • Research
  • Reproducibility
  • Computer Vision
  • NLP
  • Systems
  • Python
  • MLOps
  • Geometry
  • Debugging

Recommended creative tags:

  • Fiction
  • Fragment
  • Diary
  • Reflection

docs.title: Research Post Template
docs.text:
# Day 000: Paper Title

<p class="post-meta">Research · Reproducibility · Topic</p>

<div class="tag-row">
  <span>Research</span>
  <span>Reproducibility</span>
  <span>Topic</span>
</div>

- **Title:**
- **Authors:**
- **Link:**
- **Code:**

- **Goal:**

## References

docs.title: Publishing Checklist
docs.text:
• Add the post to the right collection folder.
  • Add a card on that collection's index.md.
  • Add the post to mkdocs.yml navigation if it should appear in the left sidebar.
  • Add a featured card on docs/index.md only for work that should represent the site.
  • Run mkdocs build --strict before pushing.

docs.title: 100 Days of Research Implementation
docs.text:
Paper reproductions, implementation logs, and lessons learned

This collection is my public lab notebook for implementing research literature. Each entry should make the paper easier to understand, show what I reproduced, document what did not work, and leave behind code-level lessons I can reuse.

Research Reproducibility Machine Learning Implementation

docs.title: Progress
docs.text: Day 001 · Template Paper Implementation Template The structure I use for paper notes, reproduction plans, results, and takeaways.

docs.title: Entry Format
docs.text:
Every paper implementation should answer the same core questions:

  • What problem does the paper solve?
  • What is the central idea?
  • What did I implement?
  • Which results did I reproduce?
  • What broke, surprised me, or changed my understanding?
  • What reusable engineering lesson came out of it?

docs.title: Day 001: Paper Implementation Template
docs.text:
Research · Reproducibility · Implementation

This is the working template for the 100-day research implementation series. I can duplicate this file for each paper, rename it with the day number and paper slug, then fill in the sections as the implementation develops.

Research Paper Reading Reproduction Experiment Log

docs.title: Paper
docs.text:
• Title: Paper title
  • Authors: Author list
  • Link: Paper URL
  • Code: Official implementation, if available
  • Dataset: Dataset or benchmark
  • Goal: What I want to reproduce

docs.title: Why This Paper?
docs.text: Write the personal and technical reason for choosing the paper. This section should make the reader understand why the paper matters and why it belongs in the series.

docs.title: Core Idea
docs.text: Explain the paper in my own words. Keep this section readable enough for an engineer who has not read the paper yet, but precise enough to show the actual mechanism.

docs.title: Implementation Plan
docs.text:
• Baseline to build first
  • Components to implement
  • Training or evaluation setup
  • Metrics to track
  • Expected result from the paper

docs.title: Reproduction Log
docs.text:
Use this section as the diary while working.

Step Status Notes Read paper Planned Capture assumptions and claims Build baseline Planned Start with the smallest working version Run experiment Planned Save config, seed, and environment Compare result Planned Match against reported metric or qualitative result

docs.title: Results
docs.text: Add metrics, plots, screenshots, tables, or links to code. If the reproduction fails, document that clearly. A failed reproduction with good notes is still useful research work.

docs.title: What I Learned
docs.text:
Capture the practical lesson:

  • Modeling lesson
  • Engineering lesson
  • Debugging lesson
  • Future experiment

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Paper link
  • Code link
  • Dataset link

docs.title: Short Stories
docs.text:
Creative diary

This collection is for fiction, fragments, and reflective writing. It gives the personal site a second voice: less about proving expertise, more about keeping imagination alive beside the technical work.

Fiction Fragments Diary

docs.title: Technical Notes
docs.text:
Engineering notes, design choices, and practical ML systems thinking

These are standalone notes that do not need the structure of a research reproduction. They are where I keep sharp explanations, implementation sketches, debugging lessons, and engineering opinions worth publishing.

Machine Learning Python Computer Vision Systems Computer Vision · Geometry Monocular depth for real-world measurement Using a reference object, depth estimation, and geometry to recover approximate metric distances from one image. Python · Packaging Are you __init__ or not? A short field guide to package layout choices in Python projects.

docs.title: Monocular depth for real-world measurement
docs.text:
January 24, 2025 · 7 min read · Computer Vision

Estimating depth from a single image is exciting on its own, but it gets much more interesting when we treat it as part of a measurement system. The practical question is not just whether a model can predict a plausible depth map, but whether that depth can help us recover real dimensions from an ordinary photo.

This post sketches a simple pipeline for doing exactly that.

docs.title: Motivation
docs.text:
Suppose we want to measure the width of a table or the height of an object from one image. A monocular depth model can provide relative geometric structure, but relative depth alone is not enough. We still need a way to anchor the scene to physical units and compensate for the distortions introduced by viewpoint.

The recipe below uses a reference object plus a depth estimator to make that jump from image space to metric space.

docs.title: 1. Capture a reference object
docs.text:
Place a calibration object in the scene and photograph it as cleanly as possible. A flat plate with known dimensions works well because it gives us a stable geometric prior. In the original note, the example assumes an 8 inch by 8 inch plate.

The goal of this object is simple: it gives us something in the scene with known physical size, which we can later use to estimate a conversion factor from pixels and depth values into real units.

docs.title: 2. Predict a depth map
docs.text:
Run a monocular depth estimation model on the image. One possible choice is Marigold, though the broader idea does not depend on one specific model.

At this stage, the output is usually a dense depth map that captures relative structure across the image. The model may be excellent at ranking near versus far regions while still being imperfect in absolute scale, so we treat the prediction as useful geometry that still needs calibration.

docs.title: 3. Estimate the target tilt
docs.text:
If the reference plate is intended to be vertical and the camera is roughly level, then the difference between the predicted depth at the top and bottom of the plate tells us something about tilt.

Let the measured depth difference be:

\[ d = \| Z_{\text{bottom}} - Z_{\text{top}} \| \]

and let the true physical height of the plate be \(h\). Then an estimate of the tilt angle is:

\[ \theta = \arctan \frac{d}{h} \]

This angle gives us a rough sense of how much the object is leaning away from the ideal front-facing setup.

docs.title: Correcting perspective distortion
docs.text: Even with a good depth map, perspective distortion can ruin direct measurements. A square plate viewed at an angle no longer looks square in the image, so we need a geometric correction before we trust pixel distances.

docs.title: Homography
docs.text: A homography is a \(3 \times 3\) transformation matrix that maps one planar view to another. For flat objects, it is the natural tool for undoing perspective distortion.

docs.title: Point selection
docs.text:
Choose at least four corresponding points on the reference object:

  • their observed coordinates in the image
  • their ideal coordinates in a front-facing undistorted view

These points should define a stable planar shape, typically a rectangle or square.

docs.title: Solving for the transform
docs.text: Once the correspondences are known, solve for the homography matrix \(H\). This gives a mapping from distorted image coordinates to corrected planar coordinates.

docs.title: Applying the transform
docs.text: Apply \(H\) either to the image itself or to the selected points you care about. After rectification, the reference object should look much closer to its true proportions, which makes later measurement steps more meaningful.

docs.title: Recovering a scale factor
docs.text:
After perspective correction, compare the corrected size of the reference plate in pixels against its true dimensions.

That ratio gives a conversion factor between image measurements and physical measurements. This is the bridge between model output and real-world scale.

docs.title: Lifting pixels into 3D
docs.text:
Now suppose we want to measure the distance between two image points \((x_1, y_1)\) and \((x_2, y_2)\), with associated depth values \(Z_1\) and \(Z_2\).

Using the camera intrinsics, we can recover approximate 3D coordinates:

\[ X = \frac{(x - c_x) \times Z}{f} \] \[ Y = \frac{(y - c_y) \times Z}{f} \]

This yields:

\[ (X_1, Y_1, Z_1), \qquad (X_2, Y_2, Z_2) \]

One practical note matters here: the depth value \(Z\) should reflect any calibration correction you applied earlier. If the raw monocular prediction is only relative, you need to rescale it before treating it as physical depth.

docs.title: Final measurement
docs.text:
Once both points are represented in 3D, the measurement becomes a standard Euclidean distance:

\[ \text{Distance} = \sqrt{(X_2 - X_1)^2 + (Y_2 - Y_1)^2 + (Z_2 - Z_1)^2} \]

At that point, the original question has been transformed from "how large does this look in the photo?" into "how far apart are these recovered 3D points?"

docs.title: Closing thought
docs.text:
What I like about this setup is that it combines learned perception with classical geometry instead of asking one model to do everything. The depth model gives structure, the reference object gives scale, and projective geometry keeps the whole pipeline honest.

It is not magic, and it is not perfectly calibrated metrology. But for many practical settings, it is a very reasonable path from a single image to an approximate metric measurement.

docs.title: Are you __init__ or not?
docs.text:
January 21, 2025 · 5 min read · Python

Python packaging looks simple until it does not. One of the first design choices people run into is whether a package should include __init__.py files everywhere, or whether it should rely on implicit namespace packages instead.

Both work. They just optimize for slightly different things.

docs.title: The toy structure
docs.text:
Imagine a package called my_package with two subpackages:

my_package
    __init__.py
    subpkg1
        __init__.py
        module1.py
    subpkg2
        __init__.py
        module2.py

The question is what those __init__.py files buy us, and what changes when we remove them.

docs.title: Option A: explicit packages with __init__.py
docs.text:
With classic packages, the top-level __init__.py can define the public shape of the package:

from .subpkg1 import module1
from .subpkg2 import module2

That means importing my_package can also expose selected submodules directly. The file becomes a small control surface for package behavior rather than just a marker.

The same idea applies one level down:

# my_package/subpkg1/__init__.py
from . import module1

# my_package/subpkg2/__init__.py
from . import module2

This style is useful when:

  • you want explicit control over what importing the package does
  • you want a clear namespace boundary
  • you need package-level setup, exports, or compatibility glue

In other words, __init__.py is not just ceremony. It can be part of the API design.

docs.title: Option B: implicit namespace packages
docs.text:
Now remove every __init__.py:

my_package/
    subpkg1/
        module1.py
    subpkg2/
        module2.py

Python can still treat this structure as a package. The package exists implicitly through the directory layout itself.

This style is nice when:

  • the package layout is simple
  • you do not need import-time behavior
  • you want less boilerplate
  • parts of the package may live across multiple directories or distributions

The tradeoff is that you lose the natural place where explicit package behavior would normally live.

docs.title: Which one should you choose?
docs.text:
If you are building a small or medium project and you care about predictability, __init__.py is usually the more readable default. It makes package boundaries obvious and gives you a home for exports when the project grows.

If you want the lightest possible structure, or you are deliberately using namespace-package behavior, implicit namespaces are perfectly valid and sometimes cleaner.

docs.title: The practical rule I use
docs.text:
I treat __init__.py as the default when I want the package to feel intentional. I skip it when I genuinely want the directory tree to stay passive.

That sounds like a small distinction, but in practice it is the difference between "this package has an interface" and "this folder just happens to contain Python modules."
